title: Return on AI: How CFOs and CIOs Can Manage the Token Meter
gist: As AI moves into production, token consumption becomes a major cost center that FinOps cannot manage; BCG proposes a 'Return on AI' (RoAI) framework centered on cost-per-successful-outcome, splitting token spend into capex/opex/COGS and governing return rather than activity.

insights.claim: As AI scales from software development into sales, marketing, and operations, token bills are exploding and traditional FinOps cannot keep pace as a cost-management discipline.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: Watch for a new software category and consulting demand around AI cost governance beyond FinOps tooling.
insights.quote: The first wave of AI governance was about access. The next wave will be about economics.
insights.locator: This is the second of two articles

insights.claim: The rising use of AI agents, which consume tokens in quantity, pushes token consumption into overdrive because agents replicate large context (instructions, history, retrieved documents, tool outputs) in each loop.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: Agentic workflows are the largest and least visible driver of AI cost inflation; monitor agent loop counts as a spend signal.
insights.quote: The rising use of AI agents, which consume tokens in quantity, pushes the meter into overdrive.

insights.claim: A token bill depends on prompt length, retrieved context, output quantity, model choice, reasoning effort, tool choice, cache behavior, and the number of loops an agent runs before it stops.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: These variables are the levers any AI cost model must instrument; vendors that expose them win enterprise trust.
insights.locator: FinOps Is a Starting Point, Not an Answer

insights.claim: Token cost is not automatically exponential and can vary widely across platforms and models, so it should not be assumed to scale uncontrollably.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: Counters the panic narrative; well-designed workflows keep costs linear and manageable, favoring disciplined adopters.
insights.quote: Token cost is not automatically exponential, and it can vary widely across platforms and models.
insights.locator: Token cost is not automatically exponential

insights.claim: The key metric to track is not simply cost but cost per outcome, captured as Return on AI (RoAI) = economic return divided by the sum of the cost of human intelligence plus the cost of tokens.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: A ready-made KPI for evaluating whether an AI deployment actually creates value; use it to screen AI investments.
insights.quote: A ratio that we call return on AI (RoAI) is a good way to capture the full cost of the AI being applied

insights.claim: The right optimization target is cost per successful outcome at the required quality, latency, risk, and human-review burden, not raw token cost, since unit costs rise linearly with tokens consumed.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: Reframes AI ROI around accepted output, not activity; the denominator must be made visible before optimizing.
insights.quote: the right optimization target is cost per successful outcome at the required quality, latency, risk, and additional burden of human review

insights.claim: A weaker model that needs multiple retries can cost more per successful outcome than a stronger model that reaches the same answer quickly, inverting the assumption that cheaper models save money.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: Cost-per-outcome, not per-token price, should drive model selection; frontier models can be cheaper on hard tasks.
insights.quote: A weaker model that needs multiple retries to reach an answer can cost more per successful outcome than a stronger model that gets to the same place quickly.

themes.theme: Accounting: capex vs opex vs COGS

insights.claim: Token costs should not sit in the IT budget or a single P&L line but be split into three types: capability-building tokens as capex-like investment, internal-work tokens as operating expense, and product/customer-interaction tokens as cost of goods sold.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: How a company books AI spend signals its maturity; COGS treatment reveals real gross-margin exposure to inference.
insights.quote: Token costs do not belong in the IT budget, nor should they be assigned to a single line anywhere in the P&L.
insights.locator: A New Cost Management Operating Model

insights.claim: AI inference alters the classic SaaS cost curve because each customer interaction carries a model-running cost, eroding the near-zero marginal cost that historically made software scale so profitable.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: AI-enabled software gross margins may structurally compress versus legacy SaaS; scrutinize inference cost in AI product P&Ls.
insights.quote: AI inference alters the cost curve: each customer interaction carries an associated cost from running the model.
insights.locator: Token cost transparency is especially important

insights.claim: Companies need a workflow-level operating model that lets management do three things: see what is happening, shape the cost, and either prove value or stop the activity.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: A concrete governance blueprint; assess whether portfolio companies have workflow-level AI attribution in place.
insights.quote: see what is happening, shape the cost, and either prove value or stop (or minimize) the activity

insights.claim: Every material AI workflow should be assigned an owner, function, product-versus-internal tag, P&L line, model, provider, and intended outcome, because spending without an owner is ungoverned and without a defined outcome is unproven.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: Attribution discipline is the prerequisite to any AI cost optimization; lack of it is a red flag.
insights.quote: Spending without an owner is ungoverned. Spending without a defined outcome is unproven.

insights.claim: Many early agents exist only because they were fast to build, and structured lookups, rules-based routing, arithmetic, and deterministic policy checks should be handled by software or APIs rather than AI models.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: Routing work off AI to traditional software cuts cost and improves reliability; a bloated agent count signals waste.
insights.quote: Many early agents exist because they were fast to build, not because a model was the right tool.

insights.claim: Work should be routed by task complexity, matching low-complexity tasks with lighter or open-weight models and reserving frontier models for genuinely hard reasoning, with systems autorouting on quality, confidence, latency, and cost.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: Model routing is a core efficiency lever; supports demand for cheaper/open models and routing infrastructure.
insights.quote: Match low-complexity work with lighter or open-weight models, and reserve frontier models for genuinely hard reasoning.

insights.claim: Prompt caching and batch processing can materially reduce repeated-context and asynchronous workload costs when designed into the workflow, so standard instructions and policies should become stable, cache-friendly prompt prefixes.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: Cache-hit rate on input tokens is a measurable efficiency KPI; caching design directly protects margins.
insights.quote: Prompt caching and batch processing can materially reduce repeated-context and asynchronous workload costs when designed into the workflow.

insights.claim: User behavior is a genuine cost lever: training staff to ask for the smallest useful answer, constrain output length, and avoid pasting unnecessary documents lowers token costs, making brevity a valuable control in a metered system.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: AI literacy and interface defaults (concise output, stopping rules) are underrated cost controls worth investing in.
insights.quote: Brevity becomes a valuable cost control lever in a metered system.

insights.claim: An agentic coding workflow can produce large output at small token cost, but lines of code are an activity metric; the true numerator is code that ships and survives review, and the denominator must include specification, review, correction, testing, and approval time.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: The same AI workflow can be net-positive or net-negative depending on accepted output and human effort; measure shipped work, not volume.
insights.quote: lines of code are an activity metric. The numerator should count code that is actually shipped and survives review.

insights.claim: A service agent that closes more tickets but drives more escalations, or a marketing agent generating unused assets, is producing activity rather than return; the governance question is whether the outcome improved after counting full cost.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: Beware vendors and internal teams reporting AI 'productivity' via activity metrics that mask negative net return.
insights.quote: The key governance question is not whether AI is busy. It is whether the outcome improved after counting the full cost.
insights.locator: The same logic applies outside of engineering

insights.claim: BCG estimates companies can substantially improve AI spending efficiency over 12 months by both reducing token costs and getting more output per token, via a phased approach of quick wins (months 1-3), big rocks (months 3-6), and a longer tail (months 6-12).
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: Provides a concrete timeline for AI cost-reduction programs; quick wins are designed to self-fund larger initiatives.
insights.quote: The quick wins fund the big rocks, whose impact is compounded by a longer tail of savings.
insights.locator: Savings Can Start Now and Build Over 12 Months

insights.claim: Token costs are real, growing fast, and attracting CEO and board-level attention, so CFOs, CIOs, and CTOs need ready answers when leaders start asking.
insights.speaker: Joppe Bijlsma
insights.so_what: AI cost governance is escalating to board agendas; expect it as a recurring topic in earnings calls and capital allocation.
insights.quote: Token costs are real and growing fast. They are attracting CEO and board-level attention.
insights.locator: Token costs are real and growing fast
